canonical_summary:
For three decades, U.S. commercial launch and reentry licensing was governed by four separate regulations: 14 CFR Part 415 (expendable launch vehicle license), Part 417 (launch safety), Part 431 (reusable launch vehicle launch and reentry), and Part 435 (reentry of non-RLV vehicles). The Federal Aviation Administration replaced this prescriptive patchwork with a single performance-based regime, 14 CFR Part 450, in the Streamlined Launch and Reentry License Requirements final rule published at 85 FR 79566 on December 10, 2020 and effective March 10, 2021.

Part 450 consolidates launch and reentry into one vehicle operator license that can authorize multiple vehicle configurations, mission profiles, and launch or reentry sites. Rather than prescribing specific hardware or processes, the rule sets safety outcomes and lets operators propose a Means of Compliance, with FAA Advisory Circulars in the AC 450-series providing one accepted method for each performance-based requirement.

The transition window closed March 9, 2026, and the FAA announced on March 17, 2026 that all U.S. commercial launch operators had migrated off the legacy parts, with 14 Part 450 licenses issued since 2021. Implementation, however, exposed staffing, technical depth, and pre-application timing gaps that the Part 450 SpARC and follow-on rulemaking are now working to address.
h1: FAA Part 450 vs legacy launch licensing: the complete reference
meta_title: FAA Part 450 vs Legacy: Complete Reference
meta_description: FAA Part 450 replaced legacy 14 CFR Parts 415, 417, 431, and 435 on March 10, 2021. Operators, dates, Means of Compliance, and what changed for new entrants.

sections.heading: The legacy four-part system: 14 CFR Parts 415, 417, 431, and 435
paragraphs: Before March 10, 2021, U.S. commercial launch and reentry licensing was split across four distinct regulations in Title 14, Chapter III, Subchapter C of the Code of Federal Regulations. Each part addressed a different vehicle class or mission phase, and the structure reflected late-1990s and early-2000s assumptions that expendable boosters were the dominant configuration and that reusable launch vehicles and reentry were novel edge cases.

The four parts were paired by function. Part 415 was the licensing gate for expendable launch vehicles, and Part 417 carried the operational and technical safety obligations that a Part 415 licensee had to satisfy in flight. Part 431 combined licensing and safety for reusable launch vehicle missions into one integrated framework, and Part 435 governed reentry of a reentry vehicle that was not an RLV, incorporating much of Part 431 by reference.

subsections.heading: Part 415: Launch license for expendable launch vehicles
paragraphs: Part 415 prescribed the requirements for obtaining a launch license for an expendable launch vehicle and the post-licensing obligations a licensee had to maintain. Subpart A defined the two license types, launch-specific and launch-operator, at section 415.3, with issuance criteria at section 415.7. Subparts B through F covered pre-application consultation (including radionuclide approval at section 415.105), policy review, payload review, and safety review for launches from a Federal range and from a non-Federal launch site.

subsections.heading: Part 417: Launch safety for expendable launch vehicles
paragraphs: Part 417 contained the operational public-safety requirements applied to any orbital or suborbital ELV launched under a Part 415 license. Subpart B set launch plan and ground safety obligations at section 417.111. Subpart C governed flight safety analysis including trajectory, debris, malfunction-turn, and expected casualty analyses at section 417.107(f). Subpart D specified flight safety system requirements (typically command destruct) under section 417.107(a). Appendices A through G held the prescriptive analysis methodologies and qualification specifications that earned Part 417 its reputation for granularity. Parts 415 and 417 were promulgated together at 71 FR 50508 on August 25, 2006.

subsections.heading: Part 431: Launch and reentry of a reusable launch vehicle
paragraphs: Part 431 governed RLV missions as a single integrated launch-and-reentry event. Subpart A defined mission-specific and operator licenses at section 431.9. Subpart C contained the acceptable RLV mission risk standard at section 431.35, expressed as expected casualty thresholds across launch, on-orbit, and reentry phases. Subpart E required licensees to provide payload and flight information, including planned launch and reentry flight paths, landing sites, and contingency abort sites, not less than 60 days before each mission. Part 431 was the regulatory home for SpaceShipOne, SpaceShipTwo, and New Shepard.

subsections.heading: Part 435: Reentry of a non-RLV reentry vehicle
paragraphs: Part 435 covered the reentry case Part 431 did not address, for example a capsule returning from orbit independent of its launch vehicle. It defined reentry-specific and reentry-operator licenses (the latter valid for a two-year renewable term) at section 435.3, with issuance criteria at section 435.9, safety review at section 435.33, and payload reentry review at section 435.43. Part 435 incorporated many Part 431 technical provisions by reference.

sections.heading: What Part 450 unified
paragraphs: 14 CFR Part 450 was finalized in the Streamlined Launch and Reentry License Requirements rule published at 85 FR 79566 on December 10, 2020 and effective March 10, 2021. The rule amended 14 CFR Parts 401, 404, 413, 414, 415, 417, 420, 431, 433, 435, 437, 440, 450, and 460, and the FAA framed it as both a consolidation and a shift to performance-based regulation.

As codified in the eCFR, Part 450 "prescribes requirements for obtaining and maintaining a license to launch, reenter, or both launch and reenter, a launch or reentry vehicle." One vehicle operator license can now authorize "one or more launches or reentries using the same vehicle or family of vehicles," eliminating the legacy split between expendable and reusable regimes and between launch and reentry.
bullets: Single license covers a portfolio of vehicle configurations, mission profiles, and launch or reentry sites
Launch and reentry are governed by one rule rather than four
Site-specific licenses (previously required for each launch site) are replaced by a single multi-site authorization
Means of Compliance framework lets operators propose how to meet performance objectives rather than follow prescriptive design standards
FAA AC 450-series Advisory Circulars publish one accepted MoC per requirement but are explicitly non-exclusive

sections.heading: Prescriptive vs performance-based: the philosophical shift
paragraphs: The legacy parts told operators how to design and operate hardware. Part 417 in particular embedded its analysis methodologies and flight safety system standards directly in Appendices A through G. Reusable vehicles under Part 431 were treated with somewhat lighter prescription, but the overall regime did not fit a rapidly diversifying industry of partially reusable boosters, suborbital tourism vehicles, lifting bodies, and air-launched systems.

Part 450 inverts the model. The FAA describes the rule as one that "replaces many prescriptive regulations with performance-based rules, intended to give industry greater flexibility to develop means of compliance that meet their objectives while maintaining public safety." Operators now state the safety outcome (collective and individual risk thresholds, far-field overpressure limits, debris containment objectives) and choose how to demonstrate they will meet it.

subsections.heading: Means of Compliance and the AC 450-series
paragraphs: A Means of Compliance is the specific method an applicant proposes to satisfy a performance-based requirement in Part 450 Subpart C (Safety Requirements). The FAA's Means of Compliance Table lists current Advisory Circulars and planned issuance dates. Each AC "presents one, but not the only, acceptable means of compliance with Part 450 regulations, and the FAA should consider other MOC that an applicant may elect to present that also satisfy the entrance and exit criteria defined within the AC."

Issued AC 450-series documents include AC 450.101-1B (risk criteria), AC 450.115-1B (flight safety analysis), AC 450.117-1 (trajectory analysis), AC 450.45-2 (system safety), AC 450.173-1 (mishap plan), and AC 450.3-1 (license application).

sections.heading: Transition timeline and grandfathering
paragraphs: The transition window is governed by amendatory instructions inside 85 FR 79566. The rule's DATES block states that it is effective March 10, 2021, except for the instructions removing Parts 415, 417, 431, and 435, which are effective March 10, 2026. Sections 415.1, 431.1, and 435.1 set the grandfather scope: legacy parts apply to licenses issued before June 8, 2021, and to licenses issued on or after that date only if the FAA accepted the application under section 413.11 before June 8, 2021.

Amendatory instructions 20, 27, 44, and 54 each read "Effective March 10, 2026, remove and reserve part [415 / 417 / 431 / 435]." That is the formal sunset. The FAA announced on March 17, 2026 that the transition was complete and that no U.S. commercial launch operator remained on a legacy license, with 14 Part 450 licenses issued since the rule's effective date.
bullets: December 10, 2020: Final rule published at 85 FR 79566
March 10, 2021: Part 450 effective; legacy parts remain in force only for grandfathered licenses and applications
June 8, 2021: Cutoff for applications eligible to be processed under legacy Parts 415, 417, 431, or 435
March 9, 2026: Last day legacy licenses can authorize operations
March 10, 2026: Parts 415, 417, 431, and 435 removed and reserved
March 17, 2026: FAA announces transition complete; all operators on Part 450

sections.heading: Operator-by-operator transition
paragraphs: By the March 9, 2026 deadline, every active U.S. commercial launch operator had migrated to Part 450 or retired its legacy-licensed vehicle. The FAA's March 17, 2026 announcement listed completed transitions, and individual operator stories show how the consolidation played out in practice.

subsections.heading: SpaceX (Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, Dragon, Starship)
paragraphs: SpaceX held separate legacy vehicle operator licenses for Falcon 9 at Cape Canaveral, Kennedy LC-39A, and Vandenberg, plus a Dragon reentry license. By the deadline, Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, and Dragon were consolidated onto Part 450, with the FAA highlighting that a single authorization now covers all three Falcon sites. Starship operates under its own Part 450 license (LRLO 23-129) issued in 2023 for Boca Chica and later amended for KSC.

subsections.heading: United Launch Alliance (Atlas V, Vulcan Centaur)
paragraphs: ULA's Vulcan Centaur received its initial vehicle operator license in July 2023 ahead of its Cert-1 flight from Cape Canaveral SLC-41. The FAA's March 2026 announcement listed ULA's Atlas and Vulcan as having completed transition to Part 450, retiring the legacy Atlas V authorization.

subsections.heading: Blue Origin (New Shepard, New Glenn)
paragraphs: Blue Origin's New Glenn was the first vehicle the company licensed natively under Part 450. The FAA issued the New Glenn Part 450 license on December 27, 2024, valid for five years, authorizing orbital missions from Cape Canaveral LC-36 with downrange booster recovery on a barge in the Atlantic. New Shepard, which had operated under a legacy Part 431 RLV mission license out of Launch Site One in West Texas, was transitioned to Part 450 by the March 9, 2026 deadline.

subsections.heading: Rocket Lab (Electron, Neutron)
paragraphs: Electron launches from Rocket Lab Launch Complex 2 at Wallops are FAA-licensed; LC-1 in New Zealand is licensed separately by the New Zealand Space Agency. The FAA's March 17, 2026 announcement listed Rocket Lab Electron among completed Part 450 transitions. Neutron, in development at Wallops, is being licensed natively under Part 450.

subsections.heading: Northrop Grumman (Antares, Pegasus XL, Minotaur-C)
paragraphs: Antares 230+ flew its final mission, NG-19 (CRS-19), on August 1, 2023. Antares 330, co-developed with Firefly using domestic Miranda engines, is being licensed under Part 450 rather than amended from the legacy authorization. Pegasus XL has not flown since the TacRL-2 mission in June 2021, and the commercial Minotaur-C variant has been dormant; Northrop is not named in the FAA's March 2026 completed-transitions list because its commercial families reached end-of-life.

subsections.heading: Virgin Galactic (SpaceShipTwo, Delta-class)
paragraphs: Virgin Galactic operated VSS Unity under FAA authorization LRLO 16-092 (originally issued August 1, 2016 and amended four times) for SpaceShipTwo flights from Spaceport America. The company retired VSS Unity after the Galactic 07 flight on June 8, 2024 to focus on Delta-class vehicles, ending active operations before Part 450 transition was required. Delta-class commercial service, targeted for 2026, will require a fresh Part 450 license.

sections.heading: Lessons learned and 2025 reforms
paragraphs: The transition closed on schedule, but industry feedback from COMSTAC, the Commercial Space Federation, the Aerospace Industries Association, and Aerospace America converged on a single diagnosis: performance-based rulemaking only streamlines if the regulator has the technical depth, staffing, and inter-agency coordination to evaluate novel compliance proposals on a clock.

The COMSTAC Regulatory Working Group's July 2023 report concluded that the FAA Office of Commercial Space Transportation (AST) lacked sufficient technical expertise to assess novel Means of Compliance, which "shifts the burden to operators" and produced an iterative pre-application loop. The 180-day statutory review clock became a fiction because the pre-application phase does not count against it. CSF President Dave Cavossa testified that Part 450 implementation had created "significant regulatory burdens on U.S. industry" despite its streamlining intent.

subsections.heading: Resourcing did not scale with cadence
paragraphs: Aerospace America reported that AST received roughly $42 million of the FAA's $12.7 billion budget, with about 100 of the agency's 40,000 employees working on space, while Falcon 9 alone flew 127 times in 2024. Former AST head Wayne Monteith conceded the rollout had gone "not well" with "ambiguity in what it takes to meet the requirements," attributing it to a drafting review process that was "truncated significantly."

subsections.heading: Reentry and flight test under-specified
paragraphs: Varda's Winnebago-1 capsule launched in June 2023 and waited roughly eight months for a reentry license, prompting the FAA to require reentry licensing before launch going forward. Treatment of reentry vehicles and flight-test vehicles was explicitly flagged as a priority topic when the FAA stood up the Launch and Reentry License Requirements Improvement Aerospace Rulemaking Committee (the Part 450 SpARC) in November 2024.

subsections.heading: The Part 450 SpARC and follow-on rulemaking
paragraphs: The Part 450 SpARC was chartered to deliver recommendations within eight months of its first meeting, with a report expected in late summer 2025 to feed any subsequent rulemaking. CSF welcomed the executive order directing the Secretary of Transportation to expedite environmental reviews and remove obstacles to launch and reentry licenses, while AIA continued to push for batch licensing modeled on aviation certification analogs.

sections.heading: What this means for new entrants today
paragraphs: A new launch or reentry company applying in 2026 and beyond has exactly one regulatory regime to learn: 14 CFR Part 450. The legacy parts are removed and reserved. The application process is organized around the performance-based Subpart C safety requirements, with the operator stating a proposed Means of Compliance for each requirement, drawn from the AC 450-series or proposed independently.
bullets: Start with the FAA Part 450 Application Compliance Checklist to map every Subpart C requirement to a planned MoC
Use the FAA Means of Compliance Table to identify which requirements have an issued Advisory Circular and which still expect novel MoC proposals
Plan for pre-application coordination to dominate the schedule; the statutory 180-day review clock starts only after the FAA accepts a complete application under section 413.11
Architect the application around a portfolio (multiple vehicle configurations, mission profiles, and sites) to capture the full benefit of the single-license model
License reentry before flight, not after; the Winnebago-1 precedent now drives FAA expectations
Track the Part 450 SpARC recommendations and follow-on rulemaking, which will reshape pre-application practice and likely refine reentry and flight-test provisions

faqs.question: When did FAA Part 450 take effect and when did legacy parts end?
faqs.answer: Part 450 was published at 85 FR 79566 on December 10, 2020 and took effect March 10, 2021. The legacy parts (14 CFR Parts 415, 417, 431, and 435) remained in force for grandfathered licenses through a five-year transition window. The amendatory instructions in the rule removed and reserved those four parts effective March 10, 2026. The FAA announced on March 17, 2026 that the transition was complete and that no U.S. commercial launch operator remained on a legacy license, with 14 Part 450 licenses issued since the rule's effective date.

faqs.question: What did 14 CFR Parts 415, 417, 431, and 435 actually cover?
faqs.answer: Part 415 was the launch license for expendable launch vehicles, defining license types and issuance criteria at sections 415.3 and 415.7. Part 417 carried the operational safety obligations a Part 415 licensee had to satisfy in flight, including flight safety analysis at section 417.107(f) and flight safety system requirements at section 417.107(a). Part 431 combined licensing and safety for reusable launch vehicle launch-and-reentry missions, with the acceptable risk standard at section 431.35. Part 435 governed reentry of a non-RLV reentry vehicle, defining license types at section 435.3 and incorporating much of Part 431 by reference.

faqs.question: What is the difference between prescriptive and performance-based regulation?
faqs.answer: Prescriptive rules tell operators how to design and operate hardware in granular detail. Part 417 in particular embedded analysis methodologies, flight safety system standards, and test specifications directly in Appendices A through G. Performance-based rules, the model Part 450 adopts, define the safety outcome an operator must achieve (collective and individual risk thresholds, far-field overpressure limits, debris containment objectives) and let the operator propose how to demonstrate compliance. The FAA describes the change as giving "industry greater flexibility to develop means of compliance that meet their objectives while maintaining public safety."

faqs.question: What is a Means of Compliance under Part 450?
faqs.answer: A Means of Compliance (MoC) is the specific method an applicant proposes to satisfy a performance-based requirement in Part 450 Subpart C (Safety Requirements). FAA Advisory Circulars in the AC 450-series publish one accepted MoC per requirement, including AC 450.101-1B (risk criteria), AC 450.115-1B (flight safety analysis), AC 450.117-1 (trajectory analysis), AC 450.45-2 (system safety), AC 450.173-1 (mishap plan), and AC 450.3-1 (license application). These ACs are explicitly non-exclusive; an operator may propose a different MoC, which the FAA evaluates against the rule's safety standard.

faqs.question: Why was the June 8, 2021 cutoff important?
faqs.answer: The grandfather scope of the legacy parts is set by sections 415.1, 431.1, and 435.1, all revised by 85 FR 79566. They state that the legacy parts apply to licenses issued before June 8, 2021, and to licenses issued on or after that date only if the FAA accepted the application under section 413.11 before June 8, 2021. After that cutoff, any new applicant had to file under Part 450. June 8, 2021 was the practical end of the door into the legacy regime, even though the parts themselves remained in force for grandfathered operations until March 10, 2026.

faqs.question: Which operators were licensed under the legacy parts?
faqs.answer: Operators on legacy authorizations included SpaceX (Falcon 9 at Cape Canaveral, Kennedy LC-39A, and Vandenberg, plus Dragon reentry), United Launch Alliance (Atlas V), Northrop Grumman (Antares, Pegasus XL, Minotaur-C), Rocket Lab (Electron at Wallops LC-2), Blue Origin (New Shepard, under Part 431), and Virgin Galactic (SpaceShipTwo / VSS Unity under LRLO 16-092 issued August 1, 2016). By the March 9, 2026 deadline, every active operator had either migrated to Part 450 or retired its legacy-licensed vehicle. Northrop's commercial families reached end-of-life and were not transitioned.

faqs.question: How did SpaceX, ULA, Blue Origin, and Rocket Lab transition?
faqs.answer: SpaceX consolidated Falcon 9 (across Cape Canaveral, LC-39A, and Vandenberg), Falcon Heavy, and Dragon onto Part 450; Starship was already on its own 2023 Part 450 license (LRLO 23-129). ULA migrated both Atlas and Vulcan Centaur, which had received its initial vehicle operator license in July 2023. Blue Origin transitioned New Shepard from its legacy Part 431 license and was the first to license a new vehicle natively under Part 450 with New Glenn on December 27, 2024. Rocket Lab transitioned Electron at Wallops and is licensing Neutron natively under Part 450.

faqs.question: Why did Part 450 implementation draw industry criticism?
faqs.answer: The COMSTAC Regulatory Working Group's July 2023 report concluded that AST lacked sufficient technical expertise to evaluate novel Means of Compliance, producing iterative pre-application loops. Commercial Space Federation President Dave Cavossa testified that implementation created "significant regulatory burdens." Aerospace America reported AST received roughly $42 million of the FAA's $12.7 billion budget with about 100 employees on space, while Falcon 9 flew 127 times in 2024. Former AST head Wayne Monteith conceded the rollout had gone "not well" with "ambiguity in what it takes to meet the requirements." The 180-day statutory review clock excluded the pre-application phase, which often dominated the timeline.

faqs.question: What is the Part 450 SpARC?
faqs.answer: The Launch and Reentry License Requirements Improvement Aerospace Rulemaking Committee, known as the Part 450 SpARC, was stood up by the FAA in November 2024 to recommend refinements to the rule and its implementation. It was chartered to deliver recommendations within eight months of its first meeting, with a report expected in late summer 2025 to feed any subsequent rulemaking. The FAA explicitly flagged treatment of reentry vehicles and flight-test vehicles as priority topics, citing cases like Varda's Winnebago-1 capsule, which waited roughly eight months for a reentry license after its June 2023 launch.

faqs.question: What happens to a legacy launch license issued before June 8, 2021?
faqs.answer: It expired no later than March 9, 2026. Amendatory instructions 20, 27, 44, and 54 of 85 FR 79566 each read "Effective March 10, 2026, remove and reserve part [415 / 417 / 431 / 435]," formally sunsetting the legacy regime. The FAA confirmed on March 17, 2026 that all U.S. commercial launch operators had migrated to Part 450. Any continued operations after that date must be conducted under a Part 450 vehicle operator license. Legacy licenses cannot be renewed or extended past the March 9, 2026 cutoff.

faqs.question: Does Part 450 cover both launch and reentry?
faqs.answer: Yes. As codified in the eCFR, Part 450 "prescribes requirements for obtaining and maintaining a license to launch, reenter, or both launch and reenter, a launch or reentry vehicle." A single vehicle operator license can authorize "one or more launches or reentries using the same vehicle or family of vehicles." This eliminates the legacy split between expendable (Parts 415/417), reusable launch and reentry (Part 431), and non-RLV reentry (Part 435). The FAA has stated that operators should obtain reentry authorization before launch, a practice driven by the Varda Winnebago-1 precedent.

faqs.question: What should a new launch entrant do first?
faqs.answer: Start with the FAA Part 450 Application Compliance Checklist and map every Subpart C safety requirement to a planned Means of Compliance, using the FAA Means of Compliance Table to identify which requirements have an issued AC 450-series Advisory Circular and which still expect a novel MoC. Architect the application around a portfolio (multiple vehicle configurations, mission profiles, and launch or reentry sites) to capture the full benefit of the single-license model. Plan for pre-application coordination to dominate the schedule; the statutory 180-day review clock only starts after the FAA accepts a complete application under section 413.11.
